1: Šimʿû ʾēlay ("hear me") opens a series of three calls (vv1, 4, 7) each addressed to a different audience. The first is addressed to those who rōdĕpê tsedeq (H7291 + H6664, "pursue righteousness") and seek YHWH — covenant-seekers within the community. Habbiṭû ʾel-tsûr ḥutstabtem (H5027 + H6697, "look to the rock from which you were hewn") — the rock (tsûr) here is the quarry of origin, not primarily a stability metaphor. The same word applies to YHWH as rock in Deuteronomy 32:4 and underlies 1 Corinthians 10:4 ("that rock was Christ"). The MKT "look to the rock from which you were hewn" preserves the quarrying image — the covenant people's origins are carved out of something solid and pre-existing.
2: Habbiṭû ʾel-ʾAbrāhām ʾăbîkem ("look to Abraham your father") — the quarry of v1 is now identified: Abraham is the geological source of the covenant nation. Wĕʾel-Śārāh tĕḥôlēlkem (H2342, "to Sarah who bore you in travail") — the verb ḥûl/ḥîl (to writhe/give birth) emphasizes the birth-pain of origin. The crucial theological claim: kî-ʾeḥād qĕrāʾtîw ("for he was one when I called him") — Abraham was only one person when YHWH called him, yet YHWH wāʾărabbēhû (H7235, "multiplied him"). The logic: if YHWH could multiply one barren couple into a nation, he can multiply the remnant of exile into restoration. Romans 4:19-21 anchors faith in exactly this logic — Abraham's one-ness against impossible odds is the pattern of faith.
3: The consolation promise: kî-niḥam YHWH Tsiyyôn (H5162, "for the LORD has comforted Zion") — the same nāḥam root that opens the entire section at Isaiah 40:1 ("comfort, comfort my people," naḥămû naḥămû). The desolate places of Zion will become like ʿēden (H5731, Eden/delight) and her wilderness like gan-YHWH (H1588, "the garden of the LORD"). The garden-of-God imagery is the new creation: Revelation 22:1-2 takes up precisely this language (the river of life, the tree of life, the garden city). The restoration from exile is the first movement of a restoration-to-Eden trajectory that ends in the new Jerusalem. Śāśôn wĕśimḥāh yimmātsēʾ bāh ("joy and gladness will be found in her") — the city of mourning (Lam 1) is the city of joy restored.
4: The second audience-call: haqšîbû ʾēlay ʿammî ("pay attention to me, my people") — now addressed to the covenant community at large rather than the righteousness-seekers of v1. Kî tôrāh mēʾittî tētsēʾ (H8451 + H3318, "for Torah will go out from me") — the Law-as-instruction emanating from YHWH is the content of the good news. Wĕmišpāṭî lĕʾôr ʿammîm ʾargîaʿ (H4941 + H216, "and my justice will be a light for the peoples I will establish") — the mišpāṭ (justice/ordinance) extends to the peoples (ʿammîm, plural — Gentiles), and the light-imagery picks up 42:6 and Isaiah 49:6 ("light to the nations"). John 1:9 ("the true light that gives light to everyone") and John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world") are the fulfillment of this cosmic-light promise.
5: Qārôb tsidqî (H7138 + H6664, "my righteousness/vindication is near") — tsedeq in Deutero-Isaiah carries the force of saving-vindication, not merely moral rectitude. Yātsāʾ yišʿî (H3318 + H3468, "my salvation has gone out") — salvation as something that moves from YHWH outward into the world. The ʾiyyîm (H339, coastlands/islands) will hope in YHWH and his zĕrôaʿ (H2220, arm/strength) — the same divine-arm language that will return in v9. Matthew 12:21 cites Isaiah 42:4 for the same coastlands/nations hope fulfilled in Jesus. The "arm" that the coastlands hope in is the arm that raised Christ from the dead (Romans 8:11).
6: The cosmic transience: śĕʾû laššāmayim ʿênêkem ("lift your eyes to the heavens") and wĕhabbîṭû ʾel-hāʾārets mittāḥat ("look at the earth beneath") — both poles of the cosmos are invoked, then declared mortal. Šāmayim kĕʿāšān nāmĕlāḥû (H4414, "the heavens will vanish like smoke") — mālaḥ means to dissolve or wear away; the heavens are as permanent as morning mist. Wĕhāʾārets kabbegĕd tibleh (H1086, "the earth will wear out like a garment") — the clothing-metaphor for creation's temporality. But yĕšûʿātî lĕʿôlām tihyeh ("my salvation will be forever") — YHWH's deliverance (yĕšûʿāh) outlasts the cosmos. 2 Peter 3:10-12 describes the dissolution of the heavens by fire; Hebrews 1:11-12 quotes this passage applied to Christ: the heavens wear out like a garment, but the Son remains. The MKT "the heavens will vanish like smoke" keeps the liquid-dissolution image.
7: The third audience-call: šimʿû ʾēlay yōdĕʿê tsedeq ("hear me, you who know righteousness") — those who have internalized YHWH's character. ʿAm tôrātî bĕlibbām (H8451 + H3820, "the people in whose heart is my Torah") — the internal Torah is the covenant ideal of Jeremiah 31:33 ("I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts"). The command: ʾal-tîrĕʾû ḥerpat ʾĕnôš ("do not fear the reproach of men") — the covenant community has internalized the law yet still faces human contempt. The parallel command in Isaiah 50:7-9 ("I have set my face like flint") anticipates Christ's face-setting toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51). The Torah-in-the-heart community is the community that will not be shaken by human opinion.
8: The garment-moth simile recurs: kî ḵabeged yōḵĕlēm ʿāš (H6211, "for the moth will eat them like a garment") and ḵatsemer yōḵĕlēm sās (H5597, "the worm will eat them like wool") — the persecutors who reproach the covenant people are as perishable as cloth. But wĕtsidqātî lĕʿôlām tihyeh ("my righteousness will be forever") — YHWH's vindication endures while human reproach dissolves. Hebrews 1:11-12 quotes the garment-wearing-out imagery (from v6) and applies it to the Son's eternity: the created order is the garment that wears out, but Christ remains unchanged. The garment metaphor runs through vv6-8 as a structural thread, applying the same perishability to both the physical cosmos and the human oppressors.
9: The arm-awakening call: ʿûrî ʿûrî liḇšî-ʿōz zĕrôaʿ YHWH (H5782, "awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD!" H2220) — the repeated imperative intensifies the call; the divine arm is personified and summoned. ʿûrî kîmê qedem ("awake as in the days of old") — the appeal is to precedent: the historical exodus. Hammakket Rahab (H7294, "who struck Rahab") — Rahab is a mythic sea-monster, here = Egypt/chaos (cf. Isaiah 30:7; Job 26:12; Psalms 87:4; Isaiah 89:10). Mĕḥôlelēt tannîm (H8577, "who pierced the dragon") — the tannîm is the sea-dragon, the chaos-creature defeated at creation and at the Reed Sea. Revelation 12:7-9 takes up this dragon-defeat language for Christ's defeat of Satan. The arm that struck Rahab is the arm that raises Christ (Acts 2:24: "freed from the agony of death").
10: The Reed Sea memory: hălōʾ-ʾatt hîʾ hammĕḥarĕbet yām ("was it not you who dried up the sea") — the direct address to the divine arm continues. Mê tĕhôm rabbāh (H8415, "the waters of the great deep") — tĕhôm rabbāh is the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2, here applied to the Red Sea crossing. The crossing becomes a type of cosmic creation: dividing the chaos-waters is what YHWH does both at creation and at exodus. Šāmāh maamaqqê-yām derek lāʿĕbōr gĕʾûlîm ("who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to cross") — the redeemed (gĕʾûlîm, from gāʾal, kinsman-redeemer) pass through the dry seabed. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 applies the Red Sea crossing as a type of baptism — the exodus-passage becomes the pattern of Christ-passage.
11: The return-of-the-redeemed: ûpĕdûyê YHWH yĕšûbûn (H6299 + H7725, "and the redeemed of the LORD will return") — the verb pādāh (to ransom/redeem by payment) distinguishes this from the kinsman-redeemer (gāʾal) of v10: both redemption modes are invoked across vv10-11. Ûbāʾû Tsiyyôn bĕrinnāh ("they will come to Zion with singing") — this verse is nearly identical to Isaiah 35:10, the great return-from-exile passage. Wĕśimḥat ʿôlām ʿal-rōʾšām ("everlasting joy upon their heads") — the crown of joy as a permanent garment. Śāśôn wĕśimḥāh yassîgû ("joy and gladness will overtake them") — not pursued but overtaking: the joy catches up to the returnees. Nāsû yāgôn wāʾanāḥāh ("sorrow and sighing will flee") — the exile's sighing flees as the return-joy advances. Revelation 21:4: "He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain."
12: The divine self-disclosure: ʾānôḵî ʾānôḵî hûʾ mĕnaḥămkĕm ("I, I am he who comforts you") — the doubled personal pronoun (ʾānôḵî ʾānôḵî) and the divine hûʾ ("he") combine to form the emphatic self-identification. This is the ʾānî hûʾ formula of Isaiah 41:4; Isaiah 43:10; Isaiah 44:6; Isaiah 46:4 — the same formula transferred into the context of comfort: the God who is first-and-last, who carries Israel from birth to gray hair, is also the one who comforts. Mî-ʾattāh wattîrĕʾî mēʾĕnôš yāmût ("who are you that you are afraid of a mortal who dies?") — the rhetorical challenge: how can the community fear a human being who is as perishable as grass (v12b), when the comforter is the eternal God? The comfort of John 14:16-17 ("I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Comforter") stands in this same tradition.
13: Wattiškaḥ YHWH ʿōśekā (H7911, "you have forgotten the LORD who made you") — the verb šāḵaḥ (to forget) stands in sharp contrast to the divine lōʾ ʾeškaḥek ("I will not forget you") of Isaiah 49:15. Israel's forgetting is not matched by YHWH's forgetting — asymmetrical faithfulness is the covenant pattern. The description of YHWH the maker: nōṭeh šāmayim (H5186, "who stretched out the heavens") and yōsēd ʾārets (H3245, "who laid the foundations of the earth") — both creation acts. The fear of the oppressor's fury is called out as irrational: ûʾayyēh ḥămat hammētsîq ("and where is the wrath of the oppressor?") — the oppressor's rage is already past tense in YHWH's account.
14: Mihĕr tsōʿeh lĕhippātēaḥ (H4116 + H6808, "the crouching prisoner quickly will be released") — the exile in chains will not die in the pit. Wĕlōʾ-yiḥsar laḥmô ("and his bread will not be lacking") — the basic provision of bread: not just freedom but sustenance. The MKT "the captive exile shall speedily be released" captures the urgency of māhar (hastened). This verse is embedded in the context of YHWH as creator-comforter (vv12-13): the same God who stretched out the heavens and laid the earth's foundations will hasten the release of each individual prisoner. The grandiose and the particular are united in YHWH's care.
15: YHWH identifies himself through creation: wĕʾānôḵî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā rōgaʿ hayyām (H7280, "I am the LORD your God who stirs up the sea") — rāgaʿ means to agitate or still; the same root can mean both to disturb the sea and to calm it (cf. Job 26:12). Wayehĕmû gallāyw ("so that its waves roar") — the sea is under YHWH's command: he both stirs it and stills it. YHWH Tsĕbāʾôt šĕmô (H6635, "the LORD of Hosts is his name") — the cosmic military title: the one who commands the heavenly armies also commands the seas. Mark 4:39 ("He rebuked the wind and said to the waves, 'Peace! Be still!'" and the sea was calm) is the Christological fulfillment of the sea-controlling divine identity: what YHWH claims here, Jesus does in Galilee.
16: Wāʾāśim dĕbāray bĕpîkā (H7760 + H1697, "I have put my words in your mouth") — the prophetic commission formula, but here given to Zion/Israel as a community, not just the individual prophet. Ûbĕtsēl yādî kissîtîkā (H6738 + H3027 + H3680, "I have covered you with the shadow of my hand") — divine protection figured as overshadowing: the same image applied to the individual Servant in Isaiah 49:2 ("in the shadow of his hand he hid me"). Linṭôaʿ šāmayim wĕliyĕsōd ʾārets ("to plant the heavens and to lay the foundations of the earth") — the new-creation language: YHWH "plants" the new heavens (v16) as he stretched out the old ones. Wĕlēʾmōr lĕTsiyyôn ʿammî-ʾāttāh ("and to say to Zion, 'You are my people'") — the covenant-restoration formula that answers Hosea's reversal (lōʾ-ʿammî, "not my people," Hosea 1:9 reversed in Hosea 2:23 / Romans 9:25-26).
17: The awakening of Zion: hitʿôrĕrî hitʿôrĕrî qûmî Yĕrûšālayim (H5782, "wake up, wake up, rise up, Jerusalem") — the doubled imperative mirrors the doubled divine-arm awakening of v9 (ʿûrî ʿûrî). The mutual awakening is structural: first the divine arm is summoned (v9), then the city responds (v17). ʾăšer šātît miyyad YHWH ʾet-kôs ḥămātô ("who drank from the hand of the LORD the cup of his wrath") — kôs ḥēmāh (H3563 + H2534, "cup of wrath") is the judgment-cup Jerusalem drank at the Babylonian destruction. The cup image is critical for understanding Mark 14:36 ("take this cup from me") — Christ in Gethsemane takes the cup of divine wrath that Jerusalem had drunk, so that it may be taken from her (v22). Kôs tarʿēlāh šātît (H8653, "the cup of staggering you drank") — the double cup: both wrath and its physical effect of stupefied staggering.
18: The abandonment within the city: ʾên-mĕnahēl lāh (H5148, "there is no one to guide her") — the city is without a guide-shepherd. Mikkol-bānîm yāldāh ("of all the sons she bore") — none of her sons can lead her out of disaster. Wĕʾên maḥăzîq bĕyādāh (H2388 + H3027, "and no one to take her by the hand") — the empty hand: the covenant people abandoned without someone to grasp them and lead them. This desolation stands in contrast to YHWH who in v12 identifies himself as the comforter — the sons have failed, but the divine father has not. Isaiah 63:9 picks up the same guiding-hand image: "in his love and mercy he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old."
19: The double disaster: štayim hēnnāh qōrĕʾôt ʾôtāk ("these two things are calling to you") — two pairs of twin-disasters form four total: šôd ûšēber (H7701 + H7667, "devastation and destruction") and rāʿāb wāḥereb (H7458 + H2719, "famine and sword"). The four words for disaster cover both external military calamity (šôd) and internal structural collapse (šēber), both deprivation (rāʿāb) and violence (ḥereb). Mî yānûd lāk ("who will mourn for you?") — the rhetorical desolation: no consoler, no mourner. This verse is the depth of the lament before the turn: the city that v22 announces will receive the cup removed. The MKT "devastation and destruction, famine and sword" keeps the four Hebrew terms as two pairs to mirror the Hebrew rhetorical structure.
20: The exhausted sons: bānayik ʿullĕpû (H5968, "your sons have fainted") — the same root as swooning/fainting from exhaustion or blow. Šāḵĕbû bĕrōʾš kol-ḥûtsôt ("they lie at the head of every street") — prostrate in the public thoroughfares, the most visible spaces of the city. Kĕtôʾ bammikĕmār (H8377 + H4364, "like an antelope caught in a net") — the wild animal that cannot escape, thrashing uselessly against the trap. Mĕlēʾîm ḥămat YHWH ("full of the wrath of the LORD") — the sons are saturated with the divine wrath (the same ḥēmāh of the cup in v17); the wrath has been internalized as the visible collapse of the community. The MKT "like an antelope in a net" preserves the hunting metaphor that makes the helplessness visceral.
21: The turn: lāḵēn šimʿî-nāʾ zōʾt ʿāniyyāh ("therefore hear this, you afflicted one") — lāḵēn (therefore) marks the pivot from lament to announcement. Wĕšikōrat wĕlōʾ miyyāyin (H7910, "drunk, but not from wine") — the stupor of judgment is not the ordinary drunkenness of excess but the divine-judgment drunkenness of the cup of wrath. The phrase echoes Acts 2:15 ("these people are not drunk as you suppose") in reverse: there the disciples are accused of wine-drunkenness but are Spirit-drunk; here Jerusalem is not wine-drunk but wrath-drunk. The connection is ironic: the Spirit-filling of Pentecost replaces the wrath-cup of judgment — the same outward appearance, opposite cause and effect.
22: The cup transferred: kōh-ʾāmar ʾădōnayik YHWH ("thus says your Lord, YHWH") — the divine guarantee. Hinnēh lāqaḥtî miyyādek ʾet-kôs hatarʿēlāh (H3947 + H3563 + H8653, "behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering") — the cup is removed from Jerusalem by divine action. ʾet-qubbaʿat kôs ḥămātî lōʾ-tôsîpî lištôt-āh ʿôd ("the bowl of my wrath's cup you will no longer drink") — the double cup (kôs + qubbaʿat) is finished for Jerusalem. Mark 14:36: "Take this cup from me." Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the moment when he asks to receive what v22 promises Jerusalem — the cup Jerusalem will no longer drink is the cup the Son will drink in her place. The transfer of the cup is the mechanism of substitutionary atonement embedded in Deutero-Isaiah's comfort-poetry. The MKT "the cup of staggering" keeps the Hebrew double-noun construction.
23: The cup handed to the oppressors: wĕśamtîhā bĕyad-mĕyassayik (H3254 + H3013, "I will put it in the hand of those who afflict you") — what Jerusalem drank, her oppressors will now drink. ʾĂšer-ʾāmĕrû lĕnapšek šĕḥî wĕnaʿăbōr ("who said to you, 'Bow down so we can walk over you'") — the humiliation of conquest: the defeated are made into human pavement. Wattāśîmî kāʾārets gēwek wĕkahûts laʿōbĕrîm ("you made your back like the ground, like the street for those crossing") — the prostration of the conquered city, its citizens trampled. Revelation 6:10 (the martyrs' cry for justice) and Isaiah 14:10 (the oppressors drink the wine of God's wrath) are the eschatological fulfillment of this verse: the cup-transfer of v22 ends with the oppressors drinking the wrath that the redeemed no longer bear. The judgment is real, final, and fully divine — the one who removes the cup from Jerusalem hands it to her persecutors.
